original_abstract:
During the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4), we deployed a high-resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS) to characterize gas-phase emissions from an extensive range of biomass fuel types, including peat, agricultural residues, and domestic cooking fires. To quantify the non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) within these complex mixtures, we employed a hybrid calibration approach utilizing both gas standards and composition-sensitive, mass-dependent sensitivity curves. By integrating these measurements with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, we successfully identified—either positively or tentatively—approximately 80–96% of the total detected NMOC mass across major fuel types, including several high-molecular-weight species.

We present a comprehensive suite of new emission factors (EFs) for globally significant biomass fuels, highlighting numerous compounds that are rarely or previously unrecorded. These include critical secondary organic aerosol precursors such as furans, phenolic compounds, and aromatic hydrocarbons. Our analysis reveals that oxygenated NMOCs constitute the largest fraction of emissions across all fuel categories. Furthermore, a comparison of cooking technologies demonstrates that traditional three-stone fires produce significantly higher EFs than more advanced stove designs. Finally, we report the detection of several understudied nitrogen-containing organic compounds; these species accounted for up to 8.7% of the fuel nitrogen and may contribute substantially to new particle formation in biomass smoke plumes.
humanized_abstract:
At the 4th Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4), a high-resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS) was used to investigate gas phase emission chemistry from a wide variety of biomass fuels including peat, agricultural residues, and domestic cooking fires. Using a hybrid calibration method based on gas standards and composition-sensitive, mass-dependent sensitivity curves, quantitative non-methane organic compound (NMOC) measurements were achieved. These measurements were then combined with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to positively or tentatively identify between 80-96% of the measured NMOCs (by mass) for a variety of fuel types including many of higher molecular weight.

Here we present a new set of emission factors (EFs) for the major biomass energy sources worldwide, and for a wide range of chemicals some of which are rarely or never measured before. These include furans, phenolics and other aromatic hydrocarbons precursors of secondary organic aerosol (SOA). The majority of the emissions are identified as oxygenated Non-Methane Organic Compounds (NMOCs). The EFs for the different cooking appliances show that the older three-stone open air designs have higher EFs than the modern improved stoves. In addition to the already known species, we also identified and quantified several little studied nitrogen-containing organic compounds (NOCs) that constituted up to 8.7% of the fuel nitrogen content, and may therefore have a large impact on the newly formed particles produced in biomass smoke plumes.

These measurements were then combined with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to positively or tentatively identify between 80-96% of the measured NMOCs (by mass) for a variety of fuel types including many of higher molecular weight.

Here we present a new set of emission factors (EFs) for the major biomass energy sources worldwide, and for a wide range of chemicals some of which are rarely or never measured before. document.input:
During the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4), we deployed a high-resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS) to characterize gas-phase emissions from an extensive range of biomass fuel types, including peat, agricultural residues, and domestic cooking fires. Finally, we report the detection of several understudied nitrogen-containing organic compounds; these species accounted for up to 8.7% of the fuel nitrogen and may contribute substantially to new particle formation in biomass smoke plumes.
